document_metadata.title: Card-Based vs. Dashboard UI in Mobile Health & Performance Apps
document_metadata.subtitle: Evidence Synthesis for Product Design
document_metadata.platform_type: B2B SaaS Behavioral Intelligence Platform | Workforce Mental Health
document_metadata.target_users: High-performing professionals (PE analysts, financial services, executives)
document_metadata.key_decision: Card-based (one insight, one action) vs. dashboard (multiple metrics) for YU mobile interface

executive_summary.report_purpose: To synthesize peer-reviewed HCI research, mobile health engagement studies, eye-tracking evidence, and commercial product data from Oura, WHOOP, and Apple Health to answer whether YU's mobile interface should present health and performance data as a card-based feed or as a multi-metric dashboard
executive_summary.overall_verdict: The evidence overwhelmingly favors a card-based, progressive disclosure approach for YU's target users. YU should adopt a card-based primary interface with the dashboard available as a secondary drill-down layer.

verdict_supporting_statistics.cognitive_load_percentage: 73–76% of mobile health task time is mental processing
verdict_supporting_statistics.f_pattern_information_loss: 60–80% of displayed information is lost to F-pattern scanning

key_evidence_threads.thread_summary: Mobile health interfaces with many concurrent elements deplete working memory and slow task completion. The GOMS analysis of mHealth apps found mental operators consuming 73–76% of task time.

Layered information revelation (summary first, detail on demand) enhances comprehension, builds trust, and reduces the anchoring bias that occurs when users see raw scores before reflecting on their subjective state.
Users apply F-pattern scanning to dense dashboards (ignoring most content), but use efficient Z-pattern scanning on single-card layouts with one clear call-to-action.

Oura's October 2024 redesign condensed 5 tabs into 3, leading with a curated 'Today' card feed described as the 'Top Stories page of a news app.' WHOOP's single Recovery Score (0–100%, color-coded) maintains <3% monthly churn.

For active/busy professionals, smartwatch research shows highest satisfaction with only TWO data types simultaneously, and fitness app eye-tracking shows grid/card layouts outperform lists for visual search speed.

section_1_hci_ux_research.section_title: HCI & UX Research: Cognitive Load, Task Completion, and Return Visits

subsection_1_1.title: The Cognitive Load Problem with Dense Dashboards

studies.study_name: Cognitive Load Theory applied to mobile health (GOMS analysis)
studies.methodology: GOMS (Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules) analysis; decomposed 10 common tasks into 398 steps
studies.key_findings: 73–76% of total task execution time comprised mental operators. Of mental operators: 52.7% were 'mentally finding', 21.2% were 'mentally verifying', 16.3% were 'mentally retrieving'. 'Poor design rapidly [depletes] cognitive resources.'

studies.study_name: Information overload and mHealth behavior change (structural equation modeling study)
studies.key_findings: Information overload significantly increased perceived health severity (β=0.202, p<0.001), perceived susceptibility (β=0.222, p<0.001), and health action cues (β=0.149, p<0.001) but triggered overutilization of medical services rather than effective behavior change.

studies.study_name: BI dashboard cognitive load components (technical review)
studies.publication: International Journal for Research Trends and Innovation
studies.key_findings: Dashboard cognitive load has three components: intrinsic load (inherent data complexity), extraneous load (poor interface design), and germane load (productive load building knowledge). Goal is to minimize extraneous load and maximize germane load. Interactive drill-down, dynamic filters, and context-sensitive disclosure significantly reduce cognitive strain.

studies.study_name: eHealth design for cognitive effort reduction
studies.key_findings: Three key interface variables driving usability: page complexity, navigational simplicity, and comprehensibility. 11 of 19 design variables significantly associated with all four usability outcomes. Reducing page complexity and increasing navigational simplicity improved all usability outcomes.

studies.study_name: Integrated health visualizations in time-critical contexts (systematic review)
studies.sample_size: 46 papers reviewed; 60 guidelines synthesized; 5 core principles proposed
studies.key_findings: No specific guidelines exist for designing holistic integrated information visualizations for time-critical contexts. Synthesized 60 guidelines; most critical principle: 'overview first, zoom and filter, then details on demand' (Shneiderman's mantra).

subsection_1_1.yu_implication: For a workforce mental health platform, information overload doesn't just reduce engagement — it can actively increase health anxiety and drive counterproductive behaviors. A dashboard showing simultaneous HRV decline, elevated resting heart rate, poor sleep score, and low readiness could trigger precisely the anxiety YU aims to reduce.

studies.methodology: Participatory design with 8 clinicians at Rhode Island Hospital and UMass Memorial Health to design a wearable data dashboard for clinical use
studies.key_findings: Clinicians preferred card-based navigation. Universally preferred flow: 'aggregate information presented first, then clickable drill-down.' Bar charts outperformed radar charts. Daily aggregate views preferred over continuous raw data. Clinician quote: 'Keep the visuals very simple... they don't have a lot of time.'

Seven themes influence mobile health engagement. Most impactful: personalization (83%), reinforcement (66%), communication (57%), navigation (49%), message presentation (46%). 29-item checklist: interfaces should show 'a graphic presentation rather than too much information' and maintain 'simple screen presentation that is not overcrowded.'

whoop_retention_data.finding: Users who logged in the WHOOP Journal 10 or more times per month showed a 15% higher retention rate in 2024 — validates the card-as-daily-habit engagement model.
whoop_retention_data.metric: 15% higher retention rate for users logging 10+ times per month in 2024

section_2_progressive_disclosure.section_title: Progressive Disclosure: Trust and Engagement Over Time

subsection_2_1.title: The Science of Progressive Disclosure in Health AI

studies.study_name: Progressive disclosure in AI clinical decision support (mixed-methods study)
studies.sample_size: 8 domain experts, 8 students, 50 general users (66 total)
studies.key_findings: Progressive disclosure significantly enhanced comprehension of AI reasoning vs presenting all information upfront. Key finding: 'Excessive or poorly timed information can distract users and hinder their understanding of an AI system's actions.' Layered approach preferred by both expert and novice users.

studies.study_name: Healthcare UX trends 2025–2026 (industry analysis by Halo Lab)
studies.key_findings: Progressive disclosure identified as a core technique for modern digital health UX: 'For truly effortless care, useful techniques include progressive disclosure, intuitive layouts, and contextual prompts to minimize cognitive load.' Represents emerging industry consensus.

subsection_2_2.title: Timing of Data Revelation and User Trust

chi_2023_study.study_name: How users want wearable data presented (CHI 2023)

phase_dependent_preferences.data_preference: Historical patterns, aggregate feedback, trend visualizations
phase_dependent_preferences.implication: Data engagement is high; users want to learn and optimize

phase_dependent_preferences.data_preference: Short-term checklists, achievement cards, validated small wins
phase_dependent_preferences.implication: Raw sensor data is discouraging; users already aware and unable to act

chi_2023_study.critical_finding: Showing data users 'can't fix right now' is actively harmful to engagement and perceived self-efficacy. The preferred approach was quantifying small positive daily activities — users wanted to feel validated, not confronted.

engage_framework.description: A 2025 six-step cyclical model for sustained engagement in precision digital health, published in Frontiers in Digital Health
engage_framework.key_principles: Initial micro-engagement requires targeted messaging (not comprehensive data dumps); habit anchoring (Step 4) requires progressive revelation of data; AI-driven adaptive personalization (Step 6) must expand data exposure over time.
engage_framework.median_90day_retention: Below 10% for commercially available health apps

anchoring_problem.description: Presenting the readiness score immediately upon opening the app leads users to interpret their subjective state through the lens of the score — preventing genuine insight.
anchoring_problem.divergence_rate: Approximately 30% of cases show divergence between subjective state and readiness score
anchoring_problem.source: Community analysis from Oura Ring users (Reddit r/ouraring)

section_3_eye_tracking.section_title: Eye-Tracking and Attention: How High-Performing Users Scan Mobile Health Screens

subsection_3_1.title: F-Pattern, Z-Pattern, and Card-Focus
subsection_3_1.intro_text: Eye-tracking research has identified distinct scanning patterns that users apply to different interface types. Understanding these patterns is critical for predicting how time-constrained professionals will interact with health data on mobile screens.

scanning_patterns.user_behavior: Two short horizontal sweeps near top, then vertical scan down left margin only. Content below second sweep is rarely read.
scanning_patterns.design_implication: 60–80% of dashboard content is ignored. Most metrics below the fold are never seen.

scanning_patterns.observed_in: Sparse, visually-led screens with one clear CTA; single-insight cards
scanning_patterns.user_behavior: Top-left → top-right → diagonal → bottom-right. Entire screen surface is engaged.
scanning_patterns.design_implication: Ideal for single-card design: score top-left, insight top-right, action CTA bottom-right.

scanning_patterns.observed_in: Mobile screens with distinct visual sections or cards
scanning_patterns.user_behavior: Users scan each card boundary to assess relevance, then commit to one card for full reading.
scanning_patterns.design_implication: Card-based layouts exploit natural boundary scanning; each card is evaluated independently.

subsection_3_2.title: Mobile App Eye-Tracking Findings Relevant to Health Apps

studies.key_findings: Grid layout superior to list layout in visual search with faster search time and fewer eye fixations. Grid/card structures reduced time-to-find and cognitive effort vs scrollable lists. Time to first fixation significantly different (p < 0.05). Men preferred grid for stimulation; women preferred list for aesthetics.

Users apply different attentional maps to different design patterns. Walk-through (sequential card-by-card) patterns best predicted by specific pattern maps. Card-based sequential UX becomes increasingly efficient as users build familiarity.

Reading performance drops significantly under high-intensity movement (F(2,42)=9.909, p<0.001). Highest satisfaction with only two data types simultaneously. Bar charts outperformed all formats. Animation impaired cognition vs static (F(2,84)=32.557, p<0.001). Dark mode significantly outperformed light mode for ease of use (F(1,42)=6.468, p=0.015) and satisfaction (F(1,42)=7.243, p=0.01).

Interface design significantly modulates attention and short-term memory. Subjective attentional control showed stronger and more consistent associations with memory accuracy than physiological eye-movement indicators. A user's internal state modulates how they process health app information independent of interface design.

subsection_3_2.design_takeaway: Mobile health screens for busy, active users must minimize concurrent data elements. Two data types is the optimal ceiling. Bar charts and static displays outperform alternatives. Dark mode improves both comfort and performance.

subsection_3_3.title: Time-Constrained Professionals: What They Actually Do
subsection_3_3.target_users: PE analysts, financial services workers, executives

key_findings.finding: Nielsen Norman Group research: 78 of 93 healthcare interactions occurred on smartphones. For complex tasks users moved to desktop, but routine health monitoring always happened on mobile.
Under time pressure, users apply F-pattern scanning to dense dashboards — ignoring approximately 60–80% of visible information, typically everything below the third visible line.

CarePortal study confirmed even trained clinicians preferred aggregate-first, drill-down-second interfaces when time was constrained. Financial professionals would exhibit even stronger preference for simplified views.

section_4_platform_analysis.section_title: What Oura, WHOOP, and Apple Health Actually Did (and What Happened)
section_4_platform_analysis.intro_text: The three most significant wearable health platforms have each navigated the dashboard-versus-card decision — and their trajectories provide powerful commercial evidence.

oura_ring.subsection_title: Oura Ring: From 5-Tab Dashboard to 3-Tab 'Today' Card System

october_2024_redesign.tabs_before: 5 tabs: Home, Readiness, Sleep, Activity, Resilience
october_2024_redesign.tabs_after: 3 tabs: Today, Vitals, and My Health
october_2024_redesign.today_tab_description: The 'Top Stories' page of a news app, delivering the most timely, relevant health updates to help you navigate your day — a curated card feed, not a comprehensive dashboard.
october_2024_redesign.design_rationale: 'Members have had to sift through multiple data points to distill the need-to-know insights' — dashboard overload was the core problem.
october_2024_redesign.progressive_disclosure_structure: Today (urgent/contextual cards) → Vitals (quick overview on demand) → My Health (long-term trends, deep dive)

outcomes.other_outcomes: Activation and retention growing, support load decreasing, improved lifetime value. 'Now users actually understand and use their data.'

october_2025_redesign.description: Oura further refined the Today tab to surface the most relevant insights for daily decisions with enhanced personalization within the three-tab structure.
october_2025_redesign.vitals_tab: Provides at-a-glance views of sleep, stress, and cardiovascular trends
october_2025_redesign.my_health_tab: Offers insights into long-term well-being including habits and routines sections

navigation.before: 5 tabs: Home, Readiness, Sleep, Activity, Resilience

primary_view.before: Multiple scores and metrics visible simultaneously
primary_view.after: Curated daily card feed ('Top Stories' model)

information_flow.before: User must navigate to find relevant data

whoop.core_ux_decision: Reduce all physiological complexity to one number — a Recovery Score (0–100%, color-coded green/yellow/red)

color_coding.green: Green (67–100%): recovered and ready for strain

recovery_score.metrics_included: HRV, resting heart rate, respiratory rate, blood oxygen, skin temperature, and sleep performance

retention_evidence.monthly_churn: Under 3% monthly churn for Pro tier
retention_evidence.journal_retention_boost: Users logging WHOOP Journal 10+ times per month showed 15% higher retention in 2024

whoop_coach_impact.lifetime_value_increase: Approximately 20% increase in average customer lifetime value

whoop.design_philosophy: WHOOP's green/yellow/red system deliberately removes granularity from the primary screen. Raw biometric data is always one tap away as a secondary disclosure layer, but the daily default experience is a single actionable state — progressive disclosure at the deepest design level.
whoop.research_finding: Runners who adjusted workouts based on Recovery scores experienced fewer injuries and achieved equal fitness gains with less total training time.

apple_health.subsection_title: Apple Health: The Dashboard Approach and Its Tradeoffs
apple_health.approach: Comprehensive multi-metric dashboard prioritizing completeness over simplicity
apple_health.ipad_version_description: 'Designed to give users rich detail at a glance so they can see information from iPad, iPhone, and Apple Watch all in one place' with 'detailed interactive charts' (iPadOS 17)
apple_health.primary_role: Apple Health functions primarily as a data aggregator and secondary repository, not a daily behavioral driver.

activity_rings.description: Apple's actual primary behavioral engagement tool — a single-screen, card-like signal representing the simplest possible visualization of complex activity data.
activity_rings.rings: Three simple ring-fill indicators: Move, Exercise, Stand — representing the day's progress toward three goals

apple_health.highlights_and_trends: Apple's research focus shifted to Highlights and Trends (progressive disclosure of notable changes) in iOS 17+, acknowledging users don't engage with comprehensive data archives but do engage with surfaced insights.

cross_platform_convergence.subsection_title: Cross-Platform Design Convergence: The Industry Verdict

convergent_patterns.pattern: A primary view leading with a single summary score or curated daily highlight (not all metrics).
Progressive disclosure to secondary detailed views.
Long-term trend data accessible on demand, not prominently featured.
Personalized, contextually-relevant surfacing of insights (not static dashboards).

cross_platform_convergence.conclusion: This convergence is not coincidental — it reflects what user engagement data has forced these teams to adopt.

subsection_5_1.title: The Evidence Verdict
subsection_5_1.intro_text: The following table synthesizes the evidence across all dimensions relevant to YU's design decision. Every row favors the card-based approach for YU's specific context: time-constrained professionals using a mobile interface for daily health and performance monitoring.

evidence_verdict_table.card_based: Lower — respects working memory limits; 73–76% of task time is mental processing in complex interfaces
evidence_verdict_table.dashboard: Higher — forces concurrent processing of multiple metrics; extraneous load dominates

evidence_verdict_table.card_based: Higher for single-insight tasks; users engage with the one presented item
evidence_verdict_table.dashboard: Lower when users must locate relevant metric among many; F-pattern causes most content to be ignored

evidence_verdict_table.card_based: Faster (Oura reported ~30% reduction after shifting to card-based Today tab)
evidence_verdict_table.dashboard: Slower; depends on user familiarity and willingness to navigate

evidence_verdict_table.card_based: Higher — habit-forming when daily insight is clear and actionable
evidence_verdict_table.dashboard: Lower — overwhelming output drives 'check later' avoidance behavior

evidence_verdict_table.card_based: Builds progressively; avoids score-anchoring bias; subjective state captured first
evidence_verdict_table.dashboard: May trigger health anxiety; anchoring bias documented in Oura community

evidence_verdict_table.card_based: Z-pattern: efficient, engages full screen, single CTA is immediately located
evidence_verdict_table.dashboard: F-pattern: two sweeps near top, then left-margin only scan; most metrics never seen

evidence_verdict_table.card_based: Yes — mobile-optimized for 30-second interactions; two data types is optimal ceiling
evidence_verdict_table.dashboard: No — requires 'desktop mode' sustained attention span; poor fit for mobile

evidence_verdict_table.card_based: Oura and WHOOP retention/engagement data strongly supports; WHOOP <3% monthly churn
evidence_verdict_table.dashboard: Apple Health engagement driven by simplest feature (Activity Rings), not comprehensive dashboard

subsection_5_2.title: YU-Specific Recommendations
subsection_5_2.context: A B2B SaaS behavioral intelligence platform serving high-performing professionals, ingesting data from 400+ devices via Terra API, and delivering insights through a conversational AI agent.

recommendations.title: Morning Primary Screen = One Card, One State, One Action
recommendations.description: Show the EWMA anomaly signal (or readiness state) as a single card with color-coded status — green, yellow, or red. Keep all the SPC (statistical process control) math invisible. The entire card contains one state indicator and one CTA: 'Tell me how you feel' — the affect-labeling micro-check-in. Captures subjective state BEFORE revealing biometric data.
recommendations.key_statistics: Z-pattern screen with one CTA achieves 100% engagement with presented content, versus 20–40% for dense dashboards

recommendations.title: Progressive Disclosure for Biometric Depth
recommendations.description: After morning check-in, reveal the biometric context card: HRV delta from personal baseline, sleep score, and overnight recovery metrics. Not before the check-in. Not simultaneously with the readiness state. Protects against anchoring bias.

recommendations.title: "Insight of the Day" Card Design
recommendations.description: Surface the single most anomalous signal for that user on that day. The AI agent explains it in one sentence. One CTA: 'Understand more' or 'Take action.' Defer all other metrics to a secondary Vitals view. Inspired by Oura's 'Top Stories' model.

recommendations.title: Secondary Drill-Down Layer (Dashboard as Layer 2)
recommendations.description: YU should maintain a complete biometric dashboard — but as a secondary drill-down layer, not the primary view. One swipe or tap from the primary card reveals the full SPC time series and baseline comparison.

recommendations.title: Eye-Tracking Informed Layout
recommendations.description: Use Z-pattern layout for primary cards: score/state positioned top-left, textual insight top-right, diagonal visual flow to action CTA bottom-right. On mobile, one card fills the screen. No multi-metric grid on the primary daily screen. Adopt dark mode as the default.
